coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots decorated with TiO2 nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Yi
Li, Ming
Hu, Guanghui
Tang, Tao
Wen, Jianfeng
Li, Xinyu
Wang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) decorated with high-quantum-yield titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanocomposite are synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Photoluminescence characterization shows that S-GQDs exhibit stronger absorption peaks at ultraviolet (UV) region than GQDs. Photocatalytic activity of the obtained S-GQD/TiO2 nanocomposite reaches 70% in 8min; this activity is significantly enhanced compared with those of GQDs/TiO2 and pure TiO2. Improved UV light absorption and effective transfer of photogenerated electrons can be attributed to integration of S-GQDs with TiO2 in a cladding manner. The present work provides a new approach for development of attractive GQD-based nanocomposites with controllable structures and photocatalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Sulfur-functionalization
Photocatalytic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of GQDs, S-GQDs and S-GQDs/TiO2 composites
Photocatalytic activity and improved photocatalytic mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the synthesis of the S-GQDs and S-GQDs/TiO2 composite.
The possible photocatalyst mechanism of S-GQDs/TiO2 under UV light.

(a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GQDs (a) and S-GQDs (b). The corresponding lateral size distribution is inserted as insets. Typical high-resolution TEM image of GQDs (c) and S-GQDs (d).
TEM images for different samples. (a) TiO2 and (b) S-GQDs/TiO2. Typical high-resolution TEM image of S-GQDs/TiO2.
XRD, FTIR analysis of different samples. (a) XRD patterns of the pure TiO2 and S-GQDs/TiO2; (b) FTIR spectra of pure TiO2, S-GQDs and S-GQDs/TiO2 composites.
XPS full spectra of S-GQDs and GQDs (a). The high-resolution C 1s spectra of (b) GQDs and (c) S-GQDs. (d) High-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum of S-GQDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the (a) GQDs and (b) S-GQDs dispersed in water, and the insets are the optical photographs of the corresponding samples under the excitation with the wavelength of 360nm.

The PL spectra of (a) GQDs and (b) S-GQDs under the excitation with different wavelengths. (c) The comparison of GQDs with S-GQDs under the irradiation with 360nm wavelength. (d) The PLE spectra of S-GQDs when fixing the emission wavelength at 520nm.
UV–vis absorption spectra of TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 and S-GQDs/TiO2 composites.

(a) The relationship between the normalized MO concentrations C/C0 and the irradiation time of different photocatalysts. (b) The relationship between the logrithm of C/C0 and the irradiation time of different photocatalysts. The wavelength of the irradiation from a mercury lamp mainly concentrates at 365nm.
(a) The Recyclability of the photocatalytic decomposition of MO by S-GQDs/TiO2. (b) The repeated photocatalytic decomposition of MO for pure TiO2 and S-GQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts.
The atomic percent (at%) of GQDs and S-GQDs resulted from XPS measurement (hydrogen was not in consideration).
Quantum yield of S-GQDs using quinine sulfate as a reference.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots decorated with TiO2 nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: S-GQDs decorated with TiO2 were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. S-GQDs/TiO2 composites showed excellent photocatalytic behavior, which is significantly enhanced compared with those of GQDs/TiO2 and pure TiO2. The possible mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity of S-GQDs/TiO2 composites can be attributed to enhanced UV light absorption, which resulted in transfer of excited electron of S-GQDs. Especially, the O2-, which generated on S-GQDs, played an important role in enhancing photocatalytic activity.

Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) decorated with high-quantum-yield titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanocomposite are synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Photoluminescence characterization shows that S-GQDs exhibit stronger absorption peaks at ultraviolet (UV) region than GQDs. Photocatalytic activity of the obtained S-GQD/TiO2 nanocomposite reaches 70% in 8min; this activity is significantly enhanced compared with those of GQDs/TiO2 and pure TiO2. Improved UV light absorption and effective transfer of photogenerated electrons can be attributed to integration of S-GQDs with TiO2 in a cladding manner. The present work provides a new approach for development of attractive GQD-based nanocomposites with controllable structures and photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most effective strategies, photocatalysis attracted increasing environmental and energy concerns [1–4]. Among known photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is used as an important semiconductor material with numerous desirable properties, such as absence of toxicity, outstanding physical and chemical stability, and low-cost [5]. Despite their good performances and stabilities, TiO2-based photocatalysts present limited applications because of a wide bandgap energy (3.2eV) and high recombination rate. To improve photocatalytic activity of TiO2, many strategies were widely used and developed; such strategies include doping various ions into TiO2 lattice [6], sensitization using absorbed molecules [7–9], coupling with other semiconductors or dyes [10,11], and surface-loading by various cocatalysts [12–15]. Among the above mentioned strategies, cocatalyst-loading on photocatalyst surfaces is the most effective and facile method for enhancement of TiO2 photocatalytic ability. Although relatively high performances can be attributed to some well-known noble metal cocatalysts, such as Pt, Au, Pd, and Fe [16–19], their expensive cost restricts their wide practical applications. Therefore, highly efficient and low-cost cocatalysts should be developed for modification and improvement of photocatalytic activity of TiO2.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) received considerable attention because of their many attractive physicochemical properties and a wide range of potential applications, such as in bio-sensing [20,21], photovoltaic devices [22], and photocatalysis [23–25]. GQDs are also environmentally friendly and highly robust against harsh chemical attacks or strong ultraviolet (UV)-light irradiation. Electronic structures of GQDs can be tuned under a wide energy range by altering their size and functionalization at edge sites. Despite these conceptual advantages, pure GQDs exhibit poor catalytic property because of their large exciton binding energy [26]. Doping effectively changes electronic density of semiconductor materials and tunes their catalytic properties. In recent years, when used as powerful energy-transfer components, GQDs acted as effective electron cocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic ability by combining with semiconductor nanoparticles, such as ZnO [27], SiO2 [28], and TiO2 [29]. However, all of the reported results of GQDs as cocatalysts for enhanced photocatalytic performance and corresponding GQD/TiO2 composites exhibit relatively high photocatalytic ability under the sunlight for long periods. Few investigations focused on enhancing photocatalytic performance of GQDs/TiO2, especially for short periods and under UV light irradiation [30]. Simultaneously, further research should also study the mechanism of photocatalysis of GQDs/TiO2 with highly efficient photocatalytic activity.

The present study synthesized sulfur-doped GQDs (S-GQDs) decorated with high-quantum-yield TiO2 nanocomposites. Through a simple hydrothermal process, S-GQDs can be tightly wrapped around surfaces of TiO2 nanoparticles. Blue-fluorescence S-GQDs with high quantum yield of 57% can remain constant in water for long periods without any changes. Photocatalytic application of S-GQD/TiO2 nanocomposites was examined by degradation of methyl orange (MO). Based on the analysis of photocatalytic properties of GQD nanocomposites and pure TiO2, mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance was also discussed. These results seem to indicate that combination of considered materials possesses expanded optical absorption range and increased adsorption capacity. S-GQD/TiO2 nanocomposites can also suppress electron-hole recombination and thus enhance their catalytic activity. Our work can potentially aid environmental protection and energy conversion.

GQDs were prepared at large scale by our previous work, which used a facile hydrothermal method with cheap pyrene as precursors [31]. S-GQDs were synthesized from GQDs and powdered S via a hydrothermal route. A mixture of GQD solution (20mL, 1.0M) and powdered S (0.05g) was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and then heated at 180°C for 12h. After purification with filtering and dialysis, the solution was dried at 80°C for two days to obtain S-GQDs.

S-GQD/TiO2 composites were established by a simple hydrothermal method. A total of 0.1g Degussa P25 TiO2 was dispersed into 200mL S-GQD aqueous solution (1mg/mL) by ultrasonication (500W, 40kHz) for 30min. Then, the mixture was stirred for 30min to form a homogeneous solution. Suspension was delivered to a Teflon autoclave and then heated at 180°C for 24h. After cooling to room temperature, resultant mixture was recovered by centrifugal washing thrice at 8500rpm to collect sediments. Then, S-GQD/TiO2 composites were obtained by drying sediments at 80°C. GQD/TiO2 composites were also prepared under the same conditions for comparison.

Photocatalytic activity of obtained S-GQD/TiO2 composite was evaluated through decolorization of MO in a quartz tube under UV light irradiation of a 300W mercury lamp. The distance between lamp and quartz tube measures 10cm. Experiments were performed at room temperature as follows: 20mg S-GQD/TiO2 composite was suspended in 40mL MO solution (20ppm) without any previous treatment then stirred in the dark for 2h to ensure equilibrium between adsorption and desorption of catalyst and MO. Subsequently, the homogenous mixture was irradiated with UV lamp. Up to 4mL solution was removed from the irradiated reaction instrument per one minute and centrifuged to completely remove catalyst particles. Concentration of MO left in solution was measured by absorbance factor using an UV/Vis/near infrared (NIR) spectrometer scanning spectrophotometer. Percentage of undegraded MO was indicated as C/C0. In the present study, C represents the current concentration of MO solution, and C0 corresponds to initial value before irradiation.

Morphologies of samples were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on ESCALAB 250Xi (TMAG, USA) using an Al Ka radiation. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of all samples were recorded within 10°–80° (2θ) utilizing a Rigaku D/MAX 2550 diffractometer operated at 40kV and 100mA. Spectra were characterized by a Perkin-Elmer spectrum 100 IR Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) instrument. Absorbance spectroscopy was characterized with an UV/Vis/NIR spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, Lambda 750). All measurements were made at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of GQDs, S-GQDs and S-GQDs/TiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the growth process of S-GQDs. Under hydrothermal conditions (180°C, 12h), S-GQDs were formed using the obtained nanocrystalline GQDs with abundant OH, CO, and COOH groups on the edges and with powdered sulfur as S sources. With the existence of oxygen-containing groups, S-GQDs trained on edges as chemically active sulfur-containing groups. Fig. 1 a–d show TEM and high-resolution TEM images of GQDs and S-GQDs. These compounds are all present as classical quasi-circles of QDs. The as-prepared GQDs are well dispersed with narrow size distributions with a mean diameter of 4.5±1nm (inset of Fig. 1a). Size distribution of S-GQDs obeys a Gaussian distribution, and the most frequent size decreased (2.5±1nm, insets in Fig. 1b); this reduction was possibly caused by further decomposition during the second hydrothermal treatment (see preparation procedure in the Experimental section). High-resolution TEM images also show crystalline GQDs and S-GQDs with lattice measurement of 0.209nm; these results are consistent with those for graphite (002) plane [32–35], implying that major parts of as-prepared S-GQDs may share a similar pristine sp2 carbon networked structure [36,37].

Morphology and prime size of composites were further analyzed by TEM. Fig. 2 a shows the very thin and smooth film of TiO2 nanoparticles. After hydrothermal process, surface morphology of TiO2 nanoparticles significantly changed, as shown in by TEM spectra in Fig. 2b. S-GQDs were observed not only on the edges but also on surfaces of composites. Together with oxygen-containing functional groups of S-GQDs, hydroxyl group on the surface of TiO2 may construct and functionalize relatively stable S-GQD/TiO2 composites. Meanwhile, the combined S-GQDs with lattice fringe of 0.21nm (Fig. 2c) is consistent with above characterization of GQDs and S-GQDs, demonstrating that TiO2 nanoparticles have been modified with S-GQDs well.

Fig. 3 a shows XRD patterns of pure TiO2 and S-GQD/TiO2 samples. Observed diffraction peaks of pure TiO2 reveal anatase TiO2 and rutile TiO2. Characteristic peaks at 2θ=25.28°, 36.86°, 37.8°, 48.05°, 53.89°, 55.02°, 62.7° represent (101), (103), (004), (200), (105), (211), and (116) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively. Other peaks at 36.06°, 41.18°, and 56.74° belong to (004), (110), and (114) planes of rutile phase TiO2, which corresponds to P25. XRD patterns of S-GQD/TiO2 composites displayed no discernible change compared with that of pure TiO2. Only the intensity of diffraction increased, indicating that S-GQDs pose no influence on TiO2 crystal structure and size. FTIR spectroscopy was also carried out to further characterize all samples. As shown in Fig. 3b, the clear peak at 511cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of TiOTi bonds. Compared with that of pure TiO2, a new peak near 633cm−1 was observed in S-GQDs/TiO2 composites; this peak corresponds to the vibration of TiOC bonds and represents the absorption peak of S-GQDs, which were successfully coupled with TiO2 [38,39]. Peaks at 1597, 1379, and 989cm−1 are associated with OCO asymmetry, symmetric vibrations, and epoxy groups, respectively. FTIR results are in agreement with those of XRD.

XPS measurement was employed to determine elemental compositions, C bonding, O bonding, and S bonding configuration of S-GQDs. Fig. 4 a shows full scan XPS spectrum of S-GQDs and GQDs. The two peaks are located at 284 and 533eV correspond to C 1s and O 1s in GQDs and S-GQDs, respectively (relatively high O1s XPS signal of S-GQDs can be attributed to the presence of sulfur-containing groups). Additional peaks at 168eV (S 2p) in S-GQDs demonstrate successful doping of GQDs, as well as a signal from impurity Na+ (497eV). Table 1 shows atomic analysis results of XPS measurements. Inevitable emergence of O in GQDs resulted from nitration of pyrene. Efficiency of using powdered S for S-doping was also proven by increasing S content, which reached as high as 10.09%.

High-resolution spectrum of C 1s region of GQDs indicates the presence of carbon in three different chemical environments (Fig. 4b) corresponding to sp2C in graphene at 284.4eV, sp3C in CN and CO at 285.6 and 286.2eV, respectively, and CO at 288.1eV from carbonyls and carboxylates [40]. As shown in Fig. 4c, similar carbon-containing groups exist in addition to sp3C in CS (285eV). However, high resolution of S 2p XPS spectrum of S-GQDs clearly shows two distinct S forms: thiophene S (CSC 2p 2/3, 163.4eV; CSC 2p 1/2, 164.5eV) and oxidized S (SO, 168.0eV; −SOn-, n=2, 3, 169.0eV) [41–43]. These results indicate that S was successfully doped into graphene structure and did not form thiophene units as elemental crystals that are physically adsorbed or wrapped within S-GQDs. Instead, doped sulfur existed in two configurations: thiophene sulfur and oxide-sulfur (shown in Scheme 1).

Optical property characterization is normally used to study physical properties of carbon-based materials. Light absorption of S-GQDs were rarely investigated by previous studies [44,45]. Effects of GQDs and S-GQDs on photoabsorption ability of composites were investigated by UV–vis spectra (as shown in Fig. 5 a and b). GQDs showed a strong broad absorption band in the visible range (400–500nm); this result is similar to those of previous reports [46,47]. Except for the broad absorption band, the strongest absorption band of S-GQDs was detected at the UV region (337nm), corresponding to n→π* transition of CO bond. Notably, differences in UV–vis spectra between GQDs and S-GQDs imply that coupling of S atom with GQDs caused appearance of a strong absorption band in the UV region.

According to UV–vis absorption spectra, 310–390nm excitation wavelengths were selected for PL measurement. In general, excitation-wavelength dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity is a fundamental characteristic observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials [35]. In this experiment, both GQDs and S-GQDs exhibited excitation-independent PL behaviors (see Fig. 6 a and b). Different excitation wavelengths induced different emission intensities, but the position of emission peak slightly changed (<10nm). Emission peak of GQDs was observed at 545nm and that of S-GQDs was detected at 520nm. As shown in Fig. 6c, under 360nm excitation, maximum shift of the emission peak measured 20nm, and fluorescent color changed accordingly from bright green (GQDs) to bright blue (S-GQDs, see insets of Fig. 5a and 5b). However, in addition to these behaviors, some significant differences were observed in optical properties between S-GQDs and GQDs [48]. Considering the nearly excitation-independent emission wavelength of S-GQDs, both size and surface state of sp2 clusters in S-GQDs were expected to be uniform. As observed, S-GQDs showed a well-defined absorption band and PL band at 337 and 521nm, with a narrow full width at half maximum of 30 and 52nm, respectively, further proving the uniform size of sp2 clusters in S-GQDs. Fig. 6d shows photoluminescence (PL) excitation spectrum of S-GQDs after fixing the emission wavelength at 520nm; one of the excitation peaks was detected at 386nm, coinciding with PL results.

To further confirm the variation in optical properties by doping with S, quantum yield was calculated as a result of absorption and PL intensity under excitation of 360nm. Table 2 shows results of quantum yield of S-GQDs, with quinine sulfate as reference. Values were calculated according to the following formula:
φ=φ
r ( I
/I r )( n
2/ n
r 2)( A
r/ A ),
where φ represents quantum yield, I corresponds to measured integrated emission intensity, n stands for refractive index (1.33 for water), and A represents optical density. Subscript “ r ” refers to reference of known quantum yield. Absolute PL quantum yield reached 57%, which is remarkably higher not only in the prepared GQDs [31] but also in previous reports [49].

As shown in Fig. 7 , pure TiO2 alone exhibits strong absorption in the UV light region and no absorption in the visible field. With the incorporation of GQDs, the GQDs/TiO2 composites exhibit enhanced UV light absorption. Moreover, the S-GQDs/TiO2 display more increased UV light absorption than that of GQDs/TiO2, despite its visible light absorption change weaken. This phenomenon is consistent with the results shown in Fig. 5. Based on their optical characteristics, photocatalytic properties of S-GQDs were characterized. Lee et al. also reported that GQDs/TiO2 composites feature excellent photocatalytic performance as a result of the interaction between GQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles [50]. S-GQDs/TiO2 (Degussa P25) composite was prepared by compositing the two materials together through a hydrothermal route. Change in color (pure white to light yellow) of TiO2 aqueous solution indicated complete integration of S-GQDs with TiO2 nanoparticles (as shown in Scheme 1). This phenomenon indicates efficient usage of combining S-GQDs with catalytic TiO2 in composite systems for photocatalysts under UV light. Ratio of MO concentration (C/C0) was selected for degradation reaction to evaluate photocatalytic activity of GQDs/TiO2 composites. As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, MO does not easily self-degrades under irradiation; this property is similar to that of obtained S-GQDs. This result implies that S-GQDs exhibited nearly no photocatalytic activity under UV light. Pure TiO2 nanoparticles exhibit good photocatalytic performance under UV light irradiation. As expected, pure TiO2 degraded ca. 40% MO (Fig. 8a) in 8min. MO degradation by S-GQDs/TiO2 reached as high as 70% in 8min; this activity is better than those of TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 (50%). These results show that photocatalytic activity of S-GQDs/TiO2 strongly depends on the absorption band located at 337nm, which differs from that of GQDs, as irradiation wavelength used in this experiment mainly concentrated at UV region. This property also accounts for this ability of S-GQDs in enhancing degradation ability of pure TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity and improved photocatalytic mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the photocatalytic decomposition ability and performance stability of the S-GQDs/TiO2 composites, the recyclability of the photocatalytic decomposition of MO was also carried out, and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 9 a. After five cycles, the S-GQDs/TiO2 composites still exhibited stable photocatalytic activity, without obvious decease for the MO removal efficiency, which can be attributed to the excellent combination of S-GQDs/TiO2. In addition, the photocatalytic performance of both pure TiO2 and S-GQDs/TiO2 composites has a slightly decrease after circular test (Fig. 9b). In consideration of the excellent photocatalytic activity of S-GQDs/TiO2 composites, these results demonstrate an efficient method to modify TiO2 for enhancing photocatalytic performance, which can be attributed to the excellent electron transport properties.

Scheme 2 illustrates the possible mechanism of MO degradation. TiO2 nanocrystals accepted electrons and promoted charge separation, thus serving as n-type semi-conductors. When UV light irradiated TiO2 nanoparticles, electrons on the valence band absorbed the UV light, transferred to the conduction band, and formed electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs [28]. e−/h+ pairs then reacted with adsorbed O2/OH− to produce active oxygen (O2) and hydroxyl (OH) radicals, which subsequently caused degradation of MO. Simultaneously, S-GQDs also absorbed UV light, leading to excitation of electrons. As S-GQDs attached to surfaces of TiO2 nanoparticles, excited electrons then transferred to TiO2 nanoparticles. Whereas TiO2 functioned as catalytic-reaction activation region, S-GQDs mainly served as an excellent electron-mobility activation region for rapid and effective transfer of photogenerated electrons to rapidly promote the following interfacial catalytic reaction. This charge separation process contributes to the formation of more active oxygen radicals. Dye molecules (MO) were oxidized by these active radicals. Compared with GQDs, a strong absorption band existed in the UV region of S-GQDs. Thus, GQDs/TiO2 exhibited a relatively weak photocatalytic activity under UV light. Electrons on S-GQDs also banded together with oxygen in MO to form O2-. The generated O2- possibly played an important role in degradation of MO, thus enhancing photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Therefore, significantly improved UV photocatalytic performance of S-GQDs/TiO2 can be attributed not only to sensitizing effect of MO to TiO2 but also to interworks between S-GQDs and TiO2.

In summary, we synthesized S-GQDs decorated with TiO2 using a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method, wherein S-GQDs exhibited uniformity in size and strong PL. Quantum yield of S-GQDs reached up to 57% at an excitation wavelength of 360nm; this value is much higher than those of previous GQDs. Relative to high uniformity in both size and surface state of sp2 clusters, additional strong absorption peak of S-GQDs appeared at 337nm. Aside from optical properties of S-GQDs, S-GQDs/TiO2 composites showed excellent photocatalytic behavior. Under short periods of time, efficiency of S-GQDs/TiO2 composites presented a remarkable improvement compared with pure TiO2; the possible mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity of S-GQDs/TiO2 composites can be attributed to enhanced UV light absorption, which resulted in transfer of the excited electron of S-GQDs. Especially, the O2-, which generated from S-GQDs, played an important role in enhancing photocatalytic activity. The present report provides new insights into further improvement of photocatalytic activity for degradation of waste organic matter and hydrogen evolution from water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11764011 & 21671129 & 11364010 & 51662004), and Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (Nos. 2016GXNSFAA380008 & 2015GXNSFBA139002), P. R. China.
